content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jane T. Coleson 
Town Attorney 
Town of Fairfax 
200 I N Main Street, Suite 390 
Walnut Creek, California 94596 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-124 
Dear Ms. Coleson: 
August 3, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act'').1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Does Council Member Reed have a conflict of interest in the decision to rezone properties 
currently zoned as Highway Commercial to Central Commercial even though he owns residential 
property within 500 feet of one of the parcels subject to the zoning change? 
CONCLUSION 
No. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Council Member Reed's single family residence. 
FACTS 
You are the Town Attorney for the Town of Fairfax and you write on behalf of Council 
Member Reed. 
Fairfax is comprised of approximately 2.2 square miles of Marin County. The Town is in the 
process of considering a number of amendments to its Zoning Code to confonn the code to the 
1 The Political Refonn Acl 1s contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofT1tle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless othemise indicated. 


File No. A-16-124 
Page No. 2 
Town's General Plan, and to make other confonning and technical changes. One of the proposed 
changes would rezone all properties currently zoned as Highway Commercial (CH) to Central 
Commercial (CC). This rezoning would allow housing units to be built on the second floor in the 
CC zone without a conditional use permit (as currently required) and with reduced parking 
standards. 
A small portion of one of the parcels that would be rezoned from CH to CC is located within 
500 feet of Council Member Reed's single family residence. This property is located on Broadway 
Boulevard, a street with a mix of commercial and residential uses that runs parallel to Sir Francis 
Drake Boulevard, a major commercial conidor. The property includes a small two story office 
building, with several off street parking spaces. 
As you noted in your request, the CC zone is the retail center of the Town and the emphasis 
is on pedestrian-friendly uses, not those reliant on automobiles. As a result, this rezoning is not 
expected to increase traffic or have measurable air quality or greenhouse gas impacts. The owner of 
the property did not initiate the rezoning; it is being undertaken to confonn the Zoning Code to the 
Town's General plan 
ANALYSIS 
A public official is prohibited from making, participating in making, or using his or her 
position to influence a government decision in which the official has a financial interest. A conflict 
of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a government decision 
on a public official's interests is material. (Section 87103.) 
Financial Interest 
The interest here is real property in which Council Member Reed has an interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in 
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party 
in, or subject of, a government decision. (RebJUlation 1870 I {a).) Based on the facts provided, 
Council Member Reed's financial interests are not explicitly involved in the zoning amendments. 
For interests that are not explicitly involved, as here, the effect need not be likely to be considered 
reasonably foreseeable. "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 
1870l(b).) 
For real property not explicitly involved in a decision and pertinent to your facts, a 
foreseeable effect is material if the decision: 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 
500 feet of the property line of the official's real property ... Notwithstanding this 
prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to 


File No. A-16-124 
Page No. 3 
participate under these circumstances if the Commission detennines that there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable 
impact on the official's property;f21 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10), (11), and (12).) 
The rezoning would allow residential use on the second story of commercial properties in 
the CC zone without a conditional use pennit. The building located within 500 feet of Council 
Member Reed's residence is currently used for offices, and, as you stated in your initial request, 
there is not expected to be any increase in traffic or any measurable impact on air quality or 
greenhouse gas levels as a result of the rezoning. The rezoning would merely change the nature of 
the use allowed on the second floor of an existing structure on a street that already includes a mix of 
residential and commercial uses. (Regulation l 8702.2(a)( 11) and ( 12).) Based on the facts provided, 
and considering the nature of the zoning change, it would appear that the dec"ision will not have a 
foreseeable and material financial effect on Council Member Reed's real property interest. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
1 The 500 feet is measured in a direct line from the nearest boundary of the subject property to the nearest 
boundary of the officiafs property.

sections.question:
Does Council Member Reed have a conflict of interest in the decision to rezone properties 
currently zoned as Highway Commercial to Central Commercial even though he owns residential 
property within 500 feet of one of the parcels subject to the zoning change?
sections.conclusion:
No. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Council Member Reed's single family residence.

QUESTION: Does Council Member Reed have a conflict of interest in the decision to rezone properties 
currently zoned as Highway Commercial to Central Commercial even though he owns residential 
property within 500 feet of one of the parcels subject to the zoning change?

CONCLUSION: No. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Council Member Reed's single family residence.
